observations.recommendation:
In view of the foregoing, we reiterated that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to review, verify, and analyze the book reconciling items, particularly the unrecorded disbursements, unidentified credit memos, and the stale checks that have been outstanding for several years. Exert efforts to locate the concerned payees, determine whether these are still valid claims and that the concerned payees are still interested to claim their checks, otherwise, consider the reversion to the Unappropriated Surplus of the General Fund, especially those that have been outstanding for more than two years.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:

a. Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to verify, and analyze the noted discrepancies between the ledger and cashbook balances of the Cash in Bank – LCCA and LCTD accounts, prepare the necessary adjustments to their respective records, and henceforth ensure that reconciliation is conducted at least quarterly, so that errors, if any, are detected and adjusted promptly;

b. Municipal Accountant to: (i) prepare and submit to the Audit Team the lacking BRS for the time-deposit account under the DBP, and henceforth ensure that BRS are regularly prepared for all bank accounts; (ii) prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries to account for the valid book reconciling items, the errors noted in recording and the reversion of stale checks, for fair and accurate presentation of the affected accounts in the Financial Statements as at year-end; and (iii) prioritize the reconciliation of the deposits in transit, and henceforth ascertain that deposits are recorded in the books at the time these were actually deposited to the AGDB.

c. Municipal Treasurer: (i) send written notices to the payees one month before the check becomes stale; (ii) for stale checks already issued to the payees, immediately inform them to return the same to the Municipal Treasurer’s Office, for cancellation and issuance of the necessary check replacements thereof; (iii) for unclaimed stale checks still in his possession, immediately cancel the same and furnish the Municipal Accountant with the related original DVs and supporting documents, to facilitate adjustment in the books; and (iv) henceforth ensure that all checks issued are properly monitored, to avoid the accumulation of stale checks.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented
observations.reason:
Book reconciling items remained unadjusted in the books due to the lack of available documents as basis for the adjustment thereof, particularly those that have been outstanding for over 10 years.

Moreover, the Municipal Accountant is still trying to determine the nature of the unrecorded disbursements, thus, remained unadjusted.

The foregoing observations are reiterated in the Part II of this Report.

observations.recommendation:
In view thereof, we reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor: (a) ensure strict compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 and the CSC Memorandum Circular No. 12 s. 2012 on the grant, utilization and liquidation of cash advances, to forestall the accumulation of unsettled balances; (b) make a formal request with the Commission on Audit for the writing-off of unliquidated cash advances of those who are already deceased and those already separated from the service, whose whereabouts are unknown, pursuant to COA Circular No. 2016-005; and (c) intensify collection efforts by taking advantage of electronic means, such as emails and/or social media platforms to demand settlement from AOs with outstanding balances, who are no longer connected with the LGU, but whose whereabouts are still known.

Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant: (i) see to it that previous cash accountabilities of all officers and employees are fully settled/liquidated and duly accounted for in the books of accounts before granting additional cash advances; (ii) refrain from granting cash advances on the last working days of the year, and see to it that cash advances not used for more than two months are immediately returned/refunded to the collecting officer; (iii) recognize impairment loss and provide an allowance for impairment for dormant and long outstanding Receivable accounts, with remote chance of collection; (iv) verify and analyze the validity of the over-liquidations and the nature of the noted negative balances, and make the necessary adjustments therein, for the fair and accurate presentation of the affected accounts in the Financial Statements; and (v) regularly issue Demand Letters to AOs and personnel with unliquidated/unsettled cash advances, particularly those that have already been outstanding for several years.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented
observations.reason:
The cash advance of Hon. Marffin C. Dulay was fully liquidated in February 2024. In compliance with COA Circular No. 97-002, the Office of the Municipal Accountant prohibits the required grant of cash advances to other elected officials other than for travelling expenses.

In CY 2024, wages of job order employees are paid via ATM, which significantly reduced the grant of cash advances for payroll. The Municipal Mayor and the concerned Municipal Officials are still waiting for the respective families/relatives of the concerned deceased AOs to provide them with copies of the Death Certificates, and required supporting documents thereof, thus, the request for write-off could not be initiated.

The Office of the Municipal Accountant has prepared and delivered Demand Letters, as well as Final Notices, to employees with non-moving cash advances beyond the prescribed period of liquidation.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: (a) ensure strict compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 94-013 on the grant, utilization and liquidation of fund transfers to implementing agencies, to forestall the accumulation of outstanding balances; (b) make a formal request with the Commission on Audit for the writing-off of dormant and/or undocumented loans receivables from borrowers who are already deceased, separated from the service and whose whereabouts are unknown, and fund transfers to LGUs and NGOs/POs, pursuant to COA Circular No. 2016-005; and (c) consider withholding the salary of those employees and officers still connected with the LGU to enforce settlement thereof.

Likewise, the Municipal Mayor was recommended to instruct the: a. Municipal Accountant to: (i) conduct regular and periodic verification, analysis and validation of the existence of receivables, particularly, the loans receivable accounts, to determine its condition and ensure collectability and continuity of the loan programs; (ii) determine the nature and validity of the negative balances, and the discrepancies in the Loans Receivable – Others, and Due from NGOs/POs accounts; (iii) refrain from granting additional fund transfers to other LGUs with outstanding balances until all previous accountabilities have been settled/liquidated and accounted for in the books of accounts; (iv) recognize impairment loss and provide an allowance for impairment for dormant and long outstanding Receivable accounts, with remote chance of collection; and (v) continue sending demand letters to the concerned LGUs, NGOs/POs and other employees/institutions under the Other Receivables account, particularly, those that have been outstanding for years, requiring settlement of their accountabilities thereof; (vi) prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries to record the noted errors in recording, for fair and accurate presentation of the affected accounts in the Financial Statements and in the submitted semestral reports on the Status of Receivables; and b. Municipal Treasurer to institute appropriate collection strategy to recover the loans receivables, particularly to beneficiaries who are still connected with the LGU, those who can still be located, and still have the capacity to pay, to ensure cash availability for future proponents of the loan programs.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented
observations.reason:
The LGU managed to collect a total amount of P1,740,118.91 from the Provident Loans. Livelihood loans were already excluded in the submitted semestral reports on the Status of Fund Transfer to NGOs/POs, thus, amount presented therein as at December 31, 2024 was already reconciled with the amount reported in the Statement of Financial Position.

Demand letters were prepared and delivered to the concerned Barangays with outstanding balances. The Municipal Treasurer has been instructed to institute collection strategies. However, similar observations were noted in CY 2024 and the concerned Municipal officials have yet to submit their comments, justification and/or compliance on the matter. The foregoing observations are reiterated in the Part II of this Report.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Inventory Committee to: (a) coordinate with the Accounting Office before conducting a complete physical count of all its Inventories and PPEs, and prepare the corresponding RPCIs and RPCPPEs, and henceforth ensure that said physical count is strictly and timely conducted every year; and (b) update the existing Physical Inventory Plan based on the latest RPCPPEs, and ensure that the inventory taking is completed in accordance with the guidelines and procedures prescribed under COA Circular No 2020-006, for the one-time cleansing of PPE accounts of the LGU.

Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the: a. GSO to classify and present all inventories and PPE items in the RPCIs and RPCPPEs, into its proper Inventory and PPE accounts, respectively, complete with all the necessary information, to facilitate reconciliation with the Accounting records; b. GSO and Municipal Accountant to: (i) evaluate and analyze the discrepancies in the Inventories and PPE accounts in their respective records amounting to P63.641 million and P1.310 billion, respectively; (ii) reconcile and make the necessary adjustments to the reported Inventories and PPE account balances in the Statement of Financial Position and the submitted RPCIs and RPCPPEs; and (iii) maintain complete and updated perpetual inventory records, which shall always be reconciled, any discrepancy, shall be properly evaluated to facilitate adjustments on the affected Inventories, PPE and Expense/Government Equity accounts, for fair and correct presentation of affected accounts in the Statement of Financial Position; and c. Municipal Accountant to: (i) take appropriate action on the Liquidation Reports and/or distribution lists submitted by the concerned departments/units; (ii) prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries to account for the distributed inventory items and those already issued to end-users with RISs; and (iii) reclassify the semi-expendable items, with unit costs of below the P50,000.00 capitalization threshold, to its proper semi-expendable property/expenses/Prior Period Adjustment accounts, for fair and correct presentation of the affected accounts in the Financial Statements.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented
observations.reason: The GSO failed to coordinate with the Municipal Accounting Office before conducting physical count of its Inventories and PPEs. Reconciliation could not be facilitated due to lack of complete perpetual inventory records. The Office of the Municipal Accountant has reclassified PPE items totaling P3.114 million with unit costs below the P50,000.00 capitalization threshold to the appropriate semi-expendable inventory account. However, the physical count had not been properly coordinated and conducted and the concerned Municipal officials have yet to submit their comments, justification and/or compliance on the matter. The foregoing observations are reiterated in the Part II of this Report.

observations.recommendation:
In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer verify the noted reconciling items and prepare the necessary adjustments to their respective records, and henceforth ensure that reconciliation are conducted at least quarterly, so that errors, if any, are detected and adjusted promptly.

We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer: (a) refrain from issuing ORs for receipt of funds directly credited to the AGDB of the LGU, in accordance with COA Circular No. 2021-014; (b) record all collections and deposits only at the time these were actually collected and deposited, for fair and accurate presentation of the Cash in Local Treasury account and other affected accounts in the Financial Statement; and (c) make confirmation with the AGDB for collections directly credited to their account and report the same using the Report of Daily Collections Directly Deposited to the Agency’s Bank Account.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented
observations.reason: Reconciliation was not conducted frequently; records require tracing of past transactions covering several years. The Municipal Treasurer has discontinued issuance of ORs for funds directly credited to bank accounts and now records collections and deposits when they are actually collected and deposited. The Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer were not able to fully reconcile their respective records as they still have to trace past transactions covering several years. The foregoing observations are reiterated in the Part II of this Report.

observations.recommendation: In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to: (a) verify the validity and existence of the creditors and the corresponding payable accounts, and determine whether these are still valid and enforceable; (b) notify the concerned creditors of their outstanding claims, if found to be without valid claim, proper reversion should be made to the Unappropriated Surplus in the General Fund; (c) determine the concerned creditor under the Trust Fund books; (d) prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries for the reclassification of the amounts due to officers and employees, NGAs, other LGUs and NGOs/POs totaling P8.629 million, to their respective Payable accounts; and (e) verify and analyze the entries debiting the Receivable account despite having no check issued yet, and prepare the necessary adjustments therein, for fair and correct presentation of the affected accounts in the Financial Statements.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented
observations.reason: Unclaimed obligations totaling P36,221.00 had been outstanding for two years or more, with no actual claims/liquidations filed by the respective creditors/recipients as of December 31, 2024. Payables totaling P97,275.00 and P451,691.00 under the SEF and Trust Fund books, respectively, were not duly identified in the submitted supporting schedules. Amounts due to various employees, NGAs, and other individuals totaling P15,384,637.50 were erroneously recorded as Accounts Payable, when these should have been recorded under the appropriate payable accounts. Adjustments were made per JEV No. 24-05-05405-33 for certain items, and other corrections were effected (e.g., inter-fund transfers) but several reclassifications and clarifications remain pending. The Municipal Accountant has yet to prepare all necessary reclassification entries.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the: a. Municipal Treasurer to cause for the prompt and regular remittance of the amounts due to the concerned LGUs within the prescribed period of remittance; and b. Municipal Accountant to: (i) immediately refund/return all the unexpended balances of the completed projects to the concerned LGUs, and prepare the corresponding liquidation reports for the remaining unsettled fund transfers for specific purpose, pursuant to COA Circular No. 94-013; (ii) verify/identify the origin and details of the outstanding Due to LGUs account balances totaling P200,776.93 and prepare the corresponding supporting schedules thereof; and (iii) prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries for the noted errors in recording for fair and accurate presentation of the Due to LGUs account and the other affected accounts in the Financial Statements as at year-end.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented
observations.reason: Amounts due to concerned LGUs were not regularly remitted, increasing the prior year balance from P9,136,337.84 by P800,666.17 to P9,937,004.01 as at December 31, 2024. Certain corrections (e.g., tuition fees misposting) were made via JEVs. The Municipal Accountant promised to prepare necessary adjustments but several balances remain outstanding and certain supporting schedules are still to be provided. The concerned Municipal officials have yet to submit comments.

observations.recommendation: In view thereof, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the: a. Municipal Treasurer to cause the prompt and regular remittance of the amounts due to the concerned agencies, within the prescribed period of remittance, to avoid any penalties and interests that may be imposed on late payments; and b. Municipal Accountant to: (i) make representations with the concerned Pag-IBIG officials relative to the over-remittance on the LGU share on Pag-IBIG contributions; (ii) ensure to use the P10,000.00 maximum monthly compensation, starting February 2024, as basis for the computation of the two percent and government’s share on Pag-IBIG premium contributions, in compliance with Section 7 of RA No. 9679, as amended by HDMF Circular No. 460 dated January 15, 2024; (iii) make representations with the concerned agencies regarding the prior years’ unreconciled balances totaling P0.142 million; (iv) verify and analyze the nature of the negative balances presented in the supporting schedules, and make the necessary adjustments thereon; and (v) prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries for the noted errors in the recording of transactions for fair and accurate presentation of the affected accounts in the Financial Statements as at year-end.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented
observations.reason: Amounts due to BIR totaling P1,548,984.65 were not remitted promptly and regularly; these have been outstanding from one month up to four years. The Accounting Office remitted certain identified balances in 2024 and 2025 (January, June 2024 and May 2025) totaling P1,033,104.29. Adjustments and corrections were made for Pag-IBIG/GSIS/PhilHealth entries, and the Elected Officials approved HDMF premiums appropriations. However, representations regarding over-remittance and prior years' unreconciled balances are still pending. Negative balances totaling P115,774.69 were identified and require adjustment; Municipal Accountant committed to reconcile and adjust noted discrepancies.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, together with the HRMO: (i) revisit and update the staffing pattern plan to assess the LGUs existing organizational structure that is responsive to its mandate, pursuant to Section 76 of RA No. 7160; (ii) prioritize the hiring of regular employees for the 32 vacant plantilla positions; and (iii) evaluate the need for hiring non-permanent personnel based on the manpower requirements of the LGU to exercise prudence in government spending and to avoid unnecessary utilization of funds through possible duplication of duties, overlapping of functions and/or work redundancy. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant submit to the Audit Team the lacking supporting documents, and henceforth ensure that all related claims are properly supported with complete documentations, and cautiously verified and reviewed for any errors, before processing for payment.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented
observations.reason: The LGU was not able to update its staffing pattern plan to determine actual manpower requirements; 1,381 non-permanent personnel were employed in CY 2024. Wages/payrolls of non-permanent personnel totaling P44,984.54 were paid despite incomplete documentation. The Municipal officials committed to prepare the detailed staffing pattern in CY 2025 and to address documentation issues, but implementation remained outstanding as at report date. The foregoing observations are reiterated in Part II of this Report.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, together with the concerned Municipal Officials, provide a reasonable explanation on the noted inconsistencies in the procurement activities of the LGU, and henceforth ensure that all procurement activities undertaken by the LGU are strictly made in accordance with the provisions of the updated 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184, to ensure the regularity of the procurement process and the award of the contracts.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the: a. BAC, BAC Secretariat and the TWG to: (i) submit all the lacking Bidding Documents to the Municipal Accountant, for the eventual submission to the Audit Team; and (ii) be cautious during the bid evaluation and post-qualification phase, for proper evaluation and assessment of the eligibility as to the legal, technical and financial capacities of the prospective bidders; b. Municipal Accountant to: (i) immediately submit to the Audit Team the lacking supporting documents for verification, and henceforth, ensure that all claims are duly supported with proper and complete supporting documents, as required under the pertinent sections of COA Circular No. 2012-001, to establish the validity, regularity and propriety of the transactions, before processing for payment; and (ii) prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries for the noted errors in recording, for fair and accurate presentation of the affected accounts in the Financial Statements; c. Municipal Engineer to: (i) ensure that detailed engineering activities on all infrastructure projects are sufficiently carried-out, pursuant to Annex A of 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184; (ii) immediately notify, in writing, the concerned contractors with negative slippages of more than five percent, and take all the necessary measures to address delays during project implementation; and (iii) closely monitor the projects to ensure that these are implemented in accordance with the approved plans and specifications and completed within the contract duration, otherwise, impose liquidated damages thereon; d. General Services Officer and the Inspectorate Team for Goods to strictly ensure, during inspection, that all pertinent items have been inspected completely delivered, in accordance with the specifications stipulated in the contract/PO; e. General Services Officer to: (i) adopt the prescribed format of the DTTs, and henceforth ensure that these are duly accomplished, complete with all the necessary information, for travels of all government vehicles; and (ii) see to it that required information, such as chassis/engine number of service vehicles, among others, were duly indicated in the PARE before issuance to the intended end-users, for proper identification and monitoring of properties; and f. Municipal Treasurer to: (i) see to it that payments are duly signed by the approving officials before processing claims for payment; (ii) ensure that the payments are received by the intended payees and/or their duly authorized representatives before the release of payments thereto; and (iii) require the printed names of the claimants in the Box D portion of the DVs, to facilitate identification.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented
observations.reason: Similar observations were noted in CY 2024. The concerned Municipal officials have yet to submit their comments, justification and/or compliance on the matter. Some corrections and submissions of lacking documents were undertaken, and the Management committed to implement the recommendations, but full compliance remains outstanding as at report date. The foregoing observations are reiterated in the Part II of this Report.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor ensure that cash advances are granted only to designated Disbursing Officers and those duly bonded with the Bureau of Treasury, to avoid the risk of future non-recoverable losses.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:

a. LDRRM Officer to: (i) ensure that all priority PPAs for the current and prior years’ unexpended LDRRMF balances are duly included in the submitted LDRRMFIP, and ensure that these are reviewed regularly and updated annually, in accordance with COA Circular No. 2012-002; and (ii) see to it that the DTTs are serially numbered and properly accomplished, complete with all the required information, in accordance with COA Circular No. 77-61; and

b. Municipal Accountant to: (i) immediately submit to the Audit Team the noted lacking supporting documents, including the 43 DVs and payrolls, for verification, and henceforth ensure that claims are duly supported with complete and proper documentation, to establish the validity, regularity and propriety of the disbursements, before processing for payment; (ii) ensure prompt and complete submission of all financial documents, records and reports to the Audit Team within the prescribed period; (iii) reconcile the understatement in the reported LDRRMF utilization and the actual disbursements thereof; (iv) prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries on the noted errors in recording, for fair and accurate presentation of the affected accounts in the Financial Statements; and (v) record disbursements in the Check Disbursements Journal only when the payments are already claimed by the respective payees.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented
observations.reason: The Office of the MDRRMO submitted LDRRMF Investment Plan 2024 and supporting documents (LBP Form No. 3, MDRRMC Resolution) indicating unexpended balances from 2019-2023 totaling P11.336 million. Ten DVs and payrolls were submitted to COA supported by transmittal letters, but sixteen DVs and other documents remained unsubmitted at audit date. The DTTs and other supporting documents still show deficiencies (non-serial numbering, incomplete information). The Municipal Accountant committed to submit lacking documents and prepare adjusting journal entries, but reconciliation and full documentation remained pending as at report date.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure that all procurement activities undertaken by the LGU are strictly in accordance with the pertinent provisions of the updated 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184, to ensure the regularity of the procurement process and the award of the contracts.

We also reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require/instruct the: a. Bids and Awards Committee (BAC), BAC Secretariat and the Technical Working Group (TWG) to: (i) immediately submit all the lacking Bidding Documents to the Municipal Accountant, for the eventual submission to the Audit Team; and (ii) be cautious in the evaluation and assessment of the eligibility as to the legal, technical and financial capacities of the prospective bidders; b. Municipal Accountant to: (i) immediately submit to the Audit Team the lacking supporting documents, for verification, and henceforth, ensure that all claims are duly supported with proper and complete supporting documents, to establish the validity, propriety and regularity of the transactions, before processing for payment; and (ii) see to it that purchases of goods are duly recorded under its proper accounts, for proper monitoring and fair and accurate presentation of affected accounts in the Financial Statements; c. Municipal Engineer to closely monitor the projects to ensure that these are implemented in accordance with the approved plans and specifications and completed within the contract duration; d. Concerned departments/units to see to it that Driver’s Trip Tickets (DTTs) are serially numbered and properly accomplished, and complete with all the required information, in accordance with COA Circular No. 77-61, and henceforth ensure that these are duly accomplished for travels of all government vehicles; and e. Municipal Treasurer to see to it that disbursement vouchers (DVs) are duly signed by the approving officials before processing claims for payment and ensure that these are duly signed by the intended payees and/or their duly authorized representatives before the release of payments thereto.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented
observations.reason: Similar observations were noted in CY 2024. The concerned Municipal officials have yet to submit their comments, justification and/or compliance on the matter. The Management indicated that some lacking documents might have been misplaced and promised to submit them, but full compliance remained outstanding at report date.

balances of the affected accounts in the Financial Statements.

In view of the foregoing, we reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal
Accountant and Municipal Treasurer verify the noted reconciling items and prepare the
necessary adjustments to their respective records, and henceforth ensure that
reconciliation are conducted at least quarterly, so that errors, if any, are detected
promptly and adjusted accordingly.

In view of the foregoing, we reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal
Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to: (a) verify the validity of the corresponding
payable accounts, and if found to be valid, immediately notify the concerned creditors
of their outstanding claims. If found to be without valid claim, proper reversion should
be made to the Unappropriated Surplus in the General Fund; (b) provide in the
supporting schedules all the necessary details/nature of payables under the SEF and Trust
Fund books; and (c) prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries for the reclassification
of the amounts due to officers and employees, NGAs and various individuals totaling
P15.385 million, to their respective Payable accounts, for fair and accurate presentation of
the affected accounts in the Financial Statements.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the
concerned Municipal officials to cause for the immediate distribution of the financial
assistance to families affected by Typhoon Egay, and ensure the immediate liquidation
thereof to the City Government of Davao.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal
Accountant to: (i) cause for the prompt and regular remittance of the amounts due to
the concerned LGUs within the prescribed period of remittance; (ii) immediately
195
refund/return all the unexpended balances of the completed projects to the concerned
LGUs, and prepare the corresponding liquidation reports for the remaining unsettled fund
transfers for specific purpose, pursuant to COA Circular No. 94-013; and (iii) exhaust all
possible means to verify/identify the origin and details of the outstanding Due to LGUs
account balances totaling P0.160 million and prepare the corresponding supporting
schedules thereof.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct/require
the:
a. Municipal Treasurer, together with other concerned Municipal officials, to
facilitate the reconciliation of records with officials of the Philippine Ports
Authority for the eventual remittance of the long outstanding supervision fees,
and henceforth ensure that succeeding collections are remitted five days after the
end of each quarter, in accordance with PPA Administrative Order No. 08-2020;
b. MSWD Officer to make representations with the DSWD and clarify whether the
LGU is required to submit liquidation reports relative to the long outstanding
balance of the SEA Program; and
c. Municipal Accountant to: (i) immediately submit the necessary liquidation
199
documents, as soon as the projects have been duly completed and/or cause the
refund of the unexpended balances of the completed PPAs to the concerned SAs
or to the Bureau of Treasury, pursuant to COA Circular No. 94-013;
(ii) coordinate with the MSWD Officer regarding the result of inquiry with the
DSWD, and make the necessary adjustments thereof; and (iii) prepare the
necessary adjusting journal entry for the error noted in the recording of the
related transaction for fair and accurate presentation of the affected accounts in
the Financial Statement.

In view of the foregoing, we reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal
Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to: (a) verify and reconcile the errors noted in
recording in their books of accounts, and prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries
to reflect the correct balances of the affected accounts in the Financial Statements; and
(b) strictly monitor the reciprocal accounts by conducting regular reconciliation thereof,
to eliminate and avoid future discrepancies, if any.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor, together with the
HRMO: (i) revisit and update the staffing pattern plan to assess the LGUs existing
organizational structure that is responsive to its mandate, pursuant to Section 76 of RA
No. 7160; (ii) prioritize the hiring of regular employees for the 33 vacant plantilla
positions; and (iii) evaluate the need for hiring non-permanent personnel based on the
manpower requirements of the LGU to exercise prudence in government spending and
to avoid unnecessary utilization of funds through possible duplication of duties,
overlapping of functions and/or work redundancy.
We also reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor,
require/instruct the:
a. Municipal Accountant to: (i) submit to the Audit Team the lacking supporting
documents, and henceforth ensure that all payments to job orders, casuals and
contractual employees and consultants are properly supported with complete
documentation, pursuant to Section 4.1.3 of COA Circular No. 2012-001, before
processing claims for payment; and (ii) closely monitor the refund of the noted
overpayments; and
b. HRMO to: (i) ensure that work schedules of the non-permanent employees are
duly stipulated in the Job Order contracts/Contracts of Service, and/or services
rendered beyond the regular working days/hours, such as on weekends and
holidays, are duly supported with authority to render services thereof; and
(ii) see to it that DTRs are carefully verified and monitored, before the
preparation of the payrolls, to avoid any overpayments thereof.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor ensure that all
procurement activities undertaken by the LGU are strictly in accordance with the
pertinent provisions of the updated 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184, to ensure the
regularity of the procurement process and the award of the contracts.
We also reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor
require/instruct the:
a. BAC, BAC Secretariat and the Technical Working Group to: (i) immediately
submit all the lacking Bidding Documents to the Municipal Accountant, for the
217
eventual submission to the Audit Team; and (ii) be cautious in the evaluation and
assessment of the eligibility as to the legal, technical and financial capacities of the
prospective bidders;
b. Municipal Accountant to: (i) immediately submit to the Audit Team the lacking
supporting documents, for verification, and henceforth, ensure that all claims are
duly supported with proper and complete supporting documents, to establish the
validity, propriety and regularity of the transactions, before processing for payment;
and (ii) purchases of goods are duly recorded under its proper accounts, for
proper monitoring and fair and accurate presentation of affected accounts in the
Financial Statements;
c. Municipal Engineer to closely monitor the projects to ensure that these are
implemented in accordance with the approved plans and specifications and
completed within the contract duration;
d. Concerned departments/units to see to it that DTTs are serially numbered and
properly accomplished, and complete with all the required information, in
accordance with COA Circular No. 77-61, and henceforth ensure that these are
duly accomplished for travels of all government vehicles; and
e. Municipal Treasurer to see to it that DVs are duly signed by the approving officials
before processing claims for payment and ensure that these are duly signed by the
intended payees and/or their duly authorized representatives before the release of
payments thereto.

In view thereof, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require/instruct the:
a. concerned contractors to: (i) immediately install all the noted non-installed items
on three infrastructure projects, otherwise, refund to the LGU the equivalent
costs of deficiencies totaling P0.894 million; and (ii) provide pictures and/or
221
proofs of compliance to the Municipal Accountant for the eventual submission to

b. concerned supplier to immediately refund to the LGU the corresponding
liquidated damages for the noted delays in the delivery of one unit Wheel Loader
amounting to P67,550.00 and provide proofs of compliance to the Municipal
Accountant for the eventual submission to the Audit Team;
c. Municipal Engineer to: (i) immediately submit a copy of the approved time
extension and revised POW, for the projects, Repair of Public Market and

the related LD for the noted delays thereof and/or impose the cost deficiency to

Construction Schedule, Bar Chart, and S-Curve, showing the planned/actual

the chassis and engine numbers, for further evaluation and validation.
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant, upon submission of the
required technical documents from the concerned personnel, immediately submit the

In view of the foregoing, we reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal
Mayor require/instruct the:
a. Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant to evaluate the idle
appropriations and the unexpended balances from previously completed
projects. If no longer needed, consider consolidating said funds to augment other
priority PPAs of the LGU;
b. Municipal Budget Officer to ensure regular reconciliation of Utilization Report
with the SAAOB prepared by the Municipal Accountant prior to the preparation
and submission of the 20% DF Utilization Report, to avoid discrepancies and/or

Utilization Report as of December 31, 2024, for accurate and reliable reporting;
and
c. Municipal Accountant to immediately submit to the Audit Team the lacking
supporting documents for verification, and henceforth, ensure that all claims are
duly supported with proper and complete supporting documents, to establish the
validity, regularity and propriety of the transactions, before processing for
payment.

We reiterate our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require/instruct the
Municipal Accountant to: (i) immediately submit to the Audit Team the lacking
supporting documents, including the 16 unsubmitted DVs and payrolls, for verification,
and henceforth ensure that claims are duly supported with complete and proper
documentation, to establish the validity, regularity and propriety of the disbursements,

Reports; and (iii) prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries on the errors noted in the

In view thereof, we reiterate our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor
require the Municipal Accountant to: (i) cause the prompt and regular remittance of the
amounts due to the concerned government agencies, within the prescribed period of
remittance, to avoid any penalties and interests that may be imposed on late payments;
and (ii) make representations with the concerned agencies regarding the prior years’
unreconciled balances totaling P0.171 million; and (ii) verify and analyze the nature of
the negative balances presented in the supporting schedules, and make the necessary
adjustments thereon, for fair and accurate presentation of the affected accounts in the
Financials Statements.

In view of this, we recommended for the settlement of audit suspensions, and disallowances
and charges within 90 days and six months from receipt of the Notices of Suspension,
Disallowances, and Charges, respectively, pursuant to Section 9.4 of the Rules and
Regulations on Settlement of Accounts. The head of the agency is responsible for ensuring
that the requirements of transactions suspended in audit are complied with.
Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant record in the books of
accounts the NDs with NFDs, under the Receivables – Disallowances/Charges account, for
proper monitoring, and for fair and accurate presentation of accounts in the Statement of
Financial Position.

In view thereof, we reiterated that the Municipal Mayor and the Municipal Treasurer communicate with the concerned service provider of the RPT Computerization System for the required repair and/or system maintenance thereof, to put into good use the available System in the MTO to generate actual, updated and real-time amount of tax collectibles at any given time.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the: a. Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to review, analyze and reconcile the noted disparities and/or variances between their respective records, and make the necessary adjustments thereof, for a reliable and accurate reporting; b. Municipal Treasurer to prioritize the updating of the RPTARs, until the RPT Computerization System had been repaired, to provide the Municipal Accountant with a certified list of taxpayers showing the amounts due and collectible for each year, and facilitate the setting up of the correct RPT and SET Receivable account balances at the beginning of each year; and c. Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries on the noted errors in the recording of RPT/SET collections, for fair and accurate presentation of the affected account balances in the Financial Statements.

observations.recommendation:
In view thereof, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor:           (a) ensure strict compliance with the provisions of COA Circular Nos. 97-002 and 94-013 on the grant, utilization and liquidation of cash advances and fund transfers to implementing agencies, to forestall the accumulation of unsettled balances; (b) make a formal request with the Commission on Audit for the writing-off of unliquidated cash advances of those who are already deceased and those separated from the service whose whereabouts are unknown, pursuant to COA Circular No. 2016-005; and (c) consider withholding the salary of those employees and officers still connected with the LGU to enforce settlement thereof.  

Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor: 

limit the amount of cash advances of AOs within the maximum cash accountability covered by their Fidelity Bonds and/or instruct the Municipal Treasurer to initiate the application for the increase of the Fidelity Bonds of the concerned AOs to amounts sufficient to cover their maximum cash accountability, and henceforth conduct regular review of the fidelity bond of AOs to ensure that every AO is adequately bonded, pursuant to Section 7.1 of COA Circular No. 97-002 and Treasury Circular No. 02-009, to avoid the risk of future non-recoverable losses; and 

instruct the Municipal Accountant to: (i) issue certification that cash advances to employees and fund transfers to other LGUs have been settled/liquidated and accounted for in the books of accounts before granting cash advances;                                 (ii) regularly issue Demand Letters to AOs with unliquidated/unsettled cash advances, particularly those that have been outstanding for several years already; and      (iii) determine the nature and validity of the negative cash advance balances and the discrepancies in the Due from LGUs account, and make the necessary adjustments therein, if necessary, for fair and accurate presentation of the affected accounts in the Statement of Financial Position.
observations.managementAction:
Out of the total P12,286,652.57 prior years’ cash advances, P5,951,087.72, or 48.43 percent had been liquidated in CY 2021.

Likewise, P203,297,181.53, or 93.86 percent  of the P216,588,315.46 total  cash advances granted, had been duly liquidated in CY 2021.

Also, salary deductions have already been imposed by the Management on some Municipal officials and employees. 

Moreover, both the Internal Audit Office and the Municipal Accountant have already sent/served Demand Letters to concerned AOs for the immediate settlement of their cash accountabilities.

In addition, P2,267,583.16 of the prior years’ outstanding balance of the Due from LGUs account had been settled and/or  liquidated in CY 2021.
observations.reason:
Several accountable officers still failed to liquidate/settle their cash accountabilities as at December 31, 2021, despite the actions taken by the Management, considering that most of them were either already deceased, no longer employed in the LGU, and/or whose whereabouts could no longer be traced, thus, collection thereof could not be enforced.

Also, the Municipal Mayor failed to make the necessary request for write off of accounts, as these had been overlooked by the concerned Municipal officials. 

Due to the foregoing, similar observations were reiterated in the Part II of this Report.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure strict compliance with pertinent laws, rules and regulations on the monitoring, utilization and reporting of the status of funds and the implementation of COVID-19 related PPAs. Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
BAC to ensure that all documentary requirements are complied with before the award of the contracts, pursuant to the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184; and
Municipal Accountant to: (i) make the necessary adjustment to effect the receipt of BGCM funds and the remaining balance to the affected accounts and ensure that receipt of funds with the same nature are taken up in the books of accounts accordingly; and (ii) immediately submit to the Audit Team the lacking supporting documents, to arrive at a decision in audit, and henceforth ensure that all claims are duly supported with proper and complete supporting documents before processing for payment.

In view thereof, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor: (a) ensure strict compliance with the provisions of COA Circular Nos. 97-002 and 94-013 on the grant, utilization and liquidation of cash advances and fund transfers to implementing agencies, to forestall the accumulation of unsettled balances; (b) make a formal request with the Commission on Audit for the writing-off of unliquidated cash advances of those who are already deceased and those separated from the service whose whereabouts are unknown, pursuant to COA Circular No. 2016-005; and (c) consider withholding the salary of those employees and officers still connected with the LGU to enforce settlement thereof.
Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
limit the amount of cash advances of AOs within the maximum cash accountability covered by their Fidelity Bonds and/or instruct the Municipal Treasurer to initiate the application for the increase of the Fidelity Bonds of the concerned AOs to amounts sufficient to cover their maximum cash accountability, and henceforth conduct regular review of the fidelity bond of AOs to ensure that every AO is adequately bonded, pursuant to Section 7.1 of COA Circular No. 97-002 and Treasury Circular No. 02-009, to avoid the risk of future non-recoverable losses; and
instruct the Municipal Accountant to: (i) issue certification that cash advances to employees and fund transfers to other LGUs have been settled/liquidated and accounted for in the books of accounts before granting cash advances;               (ii) regularly issue Demand Letters to AOs with unliquidated/unsettled cash advances, particularly those that have been outstanding for several years already; and (iii) determine the nature and validity of the negative cash advance balances and the discrepancies in the Due from LGUs account, and make the necessary adjustments therein, if necessary, for fair and accurate presentation of the affected accounts in the Statement of Financial Position.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor institute the filing of appropriate legal charges against the erring officials, employees and loan proponents, as required under Section 64 of the GAAM, Volume I. Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
Municipal Accountant to (i) conduct regular and periodic verification, analysis and validation of the existence of the loans receivable accounts, to determine its condition and ensure collectability and continuity of the loan programs; and           (ii) determine the nature and validity of the negative balances, and the discrepancies in the Due from NGOs/POs account, and make the necessary adjustments therein, if necessary, for fair and accurate presentation of the affected accounts in the Statement of Financial Position; and
Municipal Treasurer to (i) institute appropriate collection procedures to recover the non-moving/dormant accounts to ensure cash availability for future beneficiaries of various loan programs; and (ii) demand/collect from the SAMVENFARA the payment of its monthly loan amortization, including penalties thereof, pursuant to the provisions of the MOA.

In view thereof, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Inventory Committee to: (i) immediately submit to the Audit Team, the Report on the Physical Count of Inventories, for verification, and henceforth ensure that the same are submitted semestrally not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year; and            (ii) prepare a Physical Inventory Plan, and ensure that the inventory taking of PPE is completed in accordance with the guidelines and procedures prescribed under COA Circular No 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020, for the cleansing of the significant discrepancies in the PPE account balances of the Municipality.
Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the GSO and Municipal Accountant to: (i) evaluate and investigate the discrepancy amounting to P96,170,262.64, and reconcile the reported PPE balances in the Statement of Financial Position and the submitted RPCPPEs; and (ii) maintain complete and updated perpetual inventory records, which shall always be reconciled, any discrepancy, shall be properly evaluated to facilitate adjustments on the affected PPE accounts, for fair and accurate presentation in the Statement of Financial Position.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to: (a) verify the validity and existence of the payable accounts, and determine whether these are still valid and enforceable; (b) notify the concerned payees of their outstanding claims, if found to be without valid claim, proper reversion should be made to the Unappropriated Surplus in the General Fund; and (c) determine the nature and validity of the negative balances, and make the necessary adjustments therein, if necessary, for fair and accurate presentation of the Payable accounts in the Statement of Financial Position.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Bids and Awards Committee and the Technical Working Group to ensure that all procurement activities undertaken by the Municipality are strictly in accordance with the provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184, particularly during the bid evaluation and post-qualification phase, for proper evaluation and assessment of the eligibility as to legal, technical and financial capacities of the prospective bidders. Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
Municipal Accountant to: (i) immediately submit to the Audit Team, the lacking supporting documents to arrive at a decision in audit, and henceforth ensure that all claims are duly supported with proper and complete supporting documents before processing for payment, as required under Section 9.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001; and (ii) prepare the necessary Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to account for the noted errors in the recording for fair and accurate presentation of the affected accounts in the Financial Statements;
Municipal Engineer to (i) ensure that the Approved Budget for the Contracts are computed in accordance with the DPWH Department Order No. 197; (ii) closely monitor the projects to ensure that these are implemented in accordance with the approved plans and specifications and completed within the contract duration, otherwise, impose liquidated damages; and (iii) ascertain that relevant information on all infrastructure projects are duly posted on the project billboards, pursuant to COA Circular No. 2013-004; and
Municipal Treasurer to require written authorizations before payments are released to claimants other than the payees, and ascertain that all DVs are duly signed by the appropriate officials before payments are made.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Engineer to:       (a) ensure that the computation of the ABC is strictly in accordance with the DPWH Department Order No. 197 and COA Resolution No. 2015-014; (b) review thoroughly the Program of Works and Plans and Specifications, see to it that all the necessary information/details are elaborately indicated therein and that quantities do not exceed the standard or the requirement for each project; and (c) provide a plausible explanation and/or justification on the ABC and contract costs exceeding the COA Cost Estimate.

We recommended for the settlement of audit suspensions, and disallowances and charges within 90 days and six months from receipt of the Notices of Suspension, Disallowances, and Charges, respectively, pursuant to Section 9.4 of the Rules and Regulations on Settlement of Accounts. The head of the agency is responsible for ensuring that the requirements of transactions suspended in audit are complied with.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct (a) the GSO to
regularly update property cards for proper upkeep and monitoring of
properties; and (b) the Municipal Accountant (i) to reconcile the accounting
books with property records; (ii) to maintain the required PPE ledger cards; and
(iii) to adjust the affected PPE accounts for their fair and accurate presentation
in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require (a) the Municipal
Accountant to recognize expense on supplies/materials already issued to the
requisitioning units pursuant to Section 114 of the Manual on the NGAS,
Volume I, for fair and accurate presentation of the accounts in the financial
statements; and (b) the GSO and the Municipal Accounting Office to maintain
detailed inventory records which should be regularly updated for proper upkeep
and monitoring of inventories and to facilitate timely verification and
reconciliation of records.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require: (a) the Municipal
Accountant to record the valid reconciling items in the books of account
pursuant to Section 3.3 of COA Circular No. 96-011 dated October 2, 1996;
(b) the Municipal Treasurer and MHO to facilitate the reconciliation of their
records for the possible production of corroborative records to establish the
validity of the reconciling items; and (c) the Municipal Accountant and
Municipal Treasurer to properly handle the fund transfers from one bank
account to another.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require (a) the concerned
accountable officers to liquidate/settle their accountabilities immediately; (b) the
Municipal Accountant (i) strictly monitor the grant, utilization and liquidation
of cash advances to ensure prompt liquidation and forestall accumulation of
unsettled balances; and (ii) prepare the necessary adjusting entry to cash
advances with negative minimal balances to Prior Years’ Adjustment account;
and (c) the Municipal Treasurer require the disbursing officer/s to use separate
cashbooks for the five General Fund sub-accounts for proper recording and
monitoring to eliminate the occurrence of negative balances.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require: (a) the Municipal
Treasurer/Finance Committee to institute appropriate collection procedures to
recover the non-moving dormant accounts to ensure cash availability for future
beneficiaries of various loan programs; and (b) the Municipal Accountant to
conduct regular and periodic verification of loan receivable accounts to
determine its conditions that would affect its collectability.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct (a) the GSO to
cause the titling of lots in the name of the Municipality to ensure ownership and
control over the properties and to avoid any legal impediments due to possible
third party claims; and (b) the Municipal Accountant check the documentary
requirements before processing payments in accordance with Section 13.0 of
COA Circular No. 2012-001.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant (a) review the
regularity and completeness of supporting documents before funds are
transferred to the barangays as required under COA Circular No. 2012-001;
and (b) refrain from granting of additional fund transfer to the concerned
barangays until all previous fund transfers have been settled/liquidated and
accounted for in the books of accounts to prevent accumulation of unliquidated
fund transfers.

We recommended that the Management, through the Accounting and
Human Resource Offices, use the maximum amount of compensation of
P5,000.00 as basis in computing the 2% government’s share on Pag-IBIG
premium contribution in compliance with Section 7 of RA No. 9679.

strictly comply with the provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 to
establish the regularity of the procurement process and award of contract;

documents prescribed under Section 9.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 before
processing payments to ensure compliance with the 2016 Revised IRR of RA

(c) the concerned offices (Accounting and BAC) to submit the required
documents as discussed in the preceding paragraphs in accordance with the

evaluation as basis for the Audit Team’s subsequent audit action; (d) the
Municipal Engineer to require the submission of the as-built plans with date
from the contractor; and (e) the Municipal Treasurer to require a written
authority, if the claimant of the checks is other than the payee.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management:

Prepare a thorough study and evaluation of the Municipality’s needs for additional personnel to avoid excessive hiring of job order and contractual employees;

Indicate the specific work assignments/job description in the contracts to facilitate comparison with the accomplishment reports to be signed and verified by the immediate supervisor to prevent incurrence of unnecessary expenses; and

Ensure that payrolls of job order and contractual employees are supported with complete documentation pursuant to the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-001.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the Management:

To provide specific guidelines/ criteria on the grant of financial assistance as to the maximum amount and the list of projects/ programs/activities that may be implemented;

To properly monitor the utilization of funds and project accomplishments of the concerned barangays; and

To record the fund transfers in the books of accounts as receivable from the concerned barangays for proper liquidation to ensure that funds are used only for the intended purpose for transparency and fair presentation of the account in the financial statements.
